ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper is an empirical study that builds on and extends existing visualization approaches (Goodfellow et al. 2015) by using 2D projections (barycentric/bilinear) and by systematically comparing multiple optimizers, switching schedules, and batch-normalization effects (Sections 3.3, 4.1–4.4). It does not propose a fundamentally new theoretical framework, but it provides new experimental perspectives and a modest methodological addition (RK2 augmentation of optimizers, A.3–A.4). These contributions amount to a moderate, non-trivial empirical extension rather than a highly novel idea, so the novelty is incremental but informative to practitioners and researchers. | B(4): The paper does not propose a fundamentally new theoretical framework but provides a notable and novel empirical perspective by extending Goodfellow et al.’s interpolation visualizations to multi-dimensional (barycentric/bilinear) projections and systematically comparing multiple optimizers (SGD, SGDM, RMSprop, Adadelta, ADAM) and their characteristic basins (Sections 3.3, 4.1–4.2). It also introduces an RK2-augmented optimizer variant and studies optimizer switching late in training and the role of batch-normalization (Sections 3.2, 4.3–4.4), which are useful empirical contributions. These are meaningful extensions beyond prior empirical work (Goodfellow, Dauphin, Choromanska et al.) but largely empirical/diagnostic rather than a major methodological breakthrough, so the novelty is notable but incremental.

It does not propose a fundamentally new theoretical framework, but it provides new experimental perspectives and a modest methodological addition (RK2 augmentation of optimizers, A.3–A.4). These contributions amount to a moderate, non-trivial empirical extension rather than a highly novel idea, so the novelty is incremental but informative to practitioners and researchers.

It also introduces an RK2-augmented optimizer variant and studies optimizer switching late in training and the role of batch-normalization (Sections 3.2, 4.3–4.4), which are useful empirical contributions. These are meaningful extensions beyond prior empirical work (Goodfellow, Dauphin, Choromanska et al.) but largely empirical/diagnostic rather than a major methodological breakthrough, so the novelty is notable but incremental.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper is generally organized into standard sections (Introduction, Related Work, Experimental Setup, Results, Conclusion) and follows a logical experimental narrative, but the stated research questions in the Introduction are not followed by a clear, concise list of contributions early on. Notation and equations are mostly usable but contain inconsistencies and minor indexing/formatting slips (e.g. awkward equation references, ∆α notation and misnumbered/misaligned equations in appendices). Figures and tables are frequently referenced (e.g. Table 1, Figures 2–7) which suggests informative visuals, but the text contains duplicated/garbled fragments (Appendix A.6) and several grammatical/typo issues (“mimimization”, “asymtotically”, “spinglass”), reducing readability and requiring careful proofreading. | B(3): The paper is generally well-organized (Introduction → Related Work → Experimental Setup → Results → Conclusion) and states its goals early, making the overall narrative easy to follow. However, presentation problems reduce clarity: there are multiple typographical and formatting issues (e.g. the garbled/repeated text in Supplement A.6, the unfinished fragment “over 13 rd.”), and some equations and references are awkwardly formatted. Figures and tables are frequently referenced but not shown in the provided excerpt, and although notation is mostly consistent, occasional sloppy formatting and duplicated lines hinder readability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally organized into standard sections (Introduction, Related Work, Experimental Setup, Results, Conclusion) and follows a logical experimental narrative, but the stated research questions in the Introduction are not followed by a clear, concise list of contributions early on. Notation and equations are mostly usable but contain inconsistencies and minor indexing/formatting slips (e.g. awkward equation references, ∆α notation and misnumbered/misaligned equations in appendices). Figures and tables are frequently referenced (e.g. Table 1, Figures 2–7) which suggests informative visuals, but the text contains duplicated/garbled fragments (Appendix A.6) and several grammatical/typo issues (“mimimization”, “asymtotically”, “spinglass”), reducing readability and requiring careful proofreading.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (Introduction → Related Work → Experimental Setup → Results → Conclusion) and states its goals early, making the overall narrative easy to follow. However, presentation problems reduce clarity: there are multiple typographical and formatting issues (e.g. the garbled/repeated text in Supplement A.6, the unfinished fragment “over 13 rd.”), and some equations and references are awkwardly formatted. Figures and tables are frequently referenced but not shown in the provided excerpt, and although notation is mostly consistent, occasional sloppy formatting and duplicated lines hinder readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main empirical claims (different optimizers land in different critical points; optimizer-characteristic basin shapes; late switches change the found minimum) are clearly stated and supported by illustrative experiments (Sections 4.1–4.3 and figures/tables). However, the experimental methodology has weaknesses: results are limited to a small set of architectures/datasets, the number of random seeds/runs and absence of statistical tests are not reported, and conclusions rely heavily on low-dimensional interpolations which can be misleading about high-dimensional geometry (interpolation-based “sharp increase ⇒ different minimum” is not a definitive proof and saddle-versus-minimum is only heuristically addressed). Mathematical descriptions (e.g. RK2 augmentation, A.3–A.4) are plausible and not evidently incorrect, but several claims are somewhat overgeneralized given the limited empirical controls and missing statistical rigor. | B(3): The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and are backed by concrete experiments (e.g. interpolation plots between initial/final weights, output-distance metric Eq.4.1 showing ~0.16 average output difference and ≈8% label disagreement; Sections 4.1–4.3 and BN/initialization experiments in A.5). The mathematical material is minimal and internally consistent (interpolation formulas, RK2 augmentation), but no formal proofs are required. However, the experimental methodology has weaknesses: the paper does not report numbers of random seeds, confidence intervals or statistical tests, tuning/selection of hyperparameters across optimizers is under-specified (A.2–A.4) and claims that points are local minima rely on 1-D/2-D interpolation rather than Hessian/spectrum analysis, which is insufficient to rule out saddles. Some conclusions are slightly overgeneralized from the limited set of architectures/datasets and the presented evidence (e.g. “every type of change resulted in a different local minimum” and implications about basin size → margin/generalization) without stronger statistical or theoretical support.

Some conclusions are slightly overgeneralized from the limited set of architectures/datasets and the presented evidence (e.g. “every type of change resulted in a different local minimum” and implications about basin size → margin/generalization) without stronger statistical or theoretical support.
